title: APPENDIX — Quick-reference summary (not for reading aloud)

lines.text: APPENDIX — Quick-reference summary (not for reading aloud)
Core idea: Xenotransplantation is borrowing an organ from a pig — and CRISPR is what finally let us do it.
A genetically edited pig grows an organ.
Surgeons transplant it into a human.
The edits make the organ look enough like "self" that the immune system tolerates it.
The unmet need: ~100,000 people on the US transplant waiting list.
17 die per day waiting.
Mostly kidney patients, with multi-year dialysis-tethered waits.

Why pigs: Anatomy (organ size and shape close to human), reproduction (six-month maturity, two litters per year of 8-12), ethics (not great apes, on the right side of the species-line that society has drawn).
Hyperacute rejection: "The immune system's instant-kill — your antibodies see alpha-gal sugar on the pig organ, complement activates, the organ dies in minutes.
" The fifty-year wall.
Caused by pre-formed anti-alpha-gal antibodies binding pig endothelium, triggering complement, leading to membrane attack complex, endothelial death, and vessel-wide thrombosis.
Alpha-gal: galactose-alpha-1,3-galactose.
The sugar pigs make and humans don't.
Lost in great apes ~25 million years ago.
The chief antigen for hyperacute rejection.
Made by an enzyme called GalT, encoded by the GGTA1 gene.

Discovery/engineering timeline: 1906 (Jaboulay's pig kidney) → 1984 (Baby Fae's baboon heart, 21 days) → 2002 (GalT-KO pig, Immerge/PPL) → 2013 (CRISPR enters the field) → 2017 (Church/Yang PERV-free pigs, 25 simultaneous knockouts) → January 2022 (Bennett, first pig heart, 60 days) → March 2024 (Slayman, first pig kidney, 49 days) → April 2024 (Pisano, kidney + thymus) → November 2024 (Looney, longest survival so far).
Triple knockout: GalT (alpha-gal) + CMAH (Neu5Gc) + β4GalNT2 (Sda).
"The GMO patient — three sugar-modifying enzymes deleted, three xenoantigens stripped off.
Human transgenes inserted: complement regulators (CD46, CD55, CD59), anticoagulants (thrombomodulin, EPCR, CD39), T-cell modulators (CD47, HLA-E).
"Make the pig look more like self.
PERVs: porcine endogenous retroviruses, ~25-50 copies per pig genome.
"Endogenous retroviruses every pig genome carries — could theoretically jump into humans during transplant; Church's 25-KO eliminated them.
" 2017 Niu/Yang/Church paper inactivated all functional PERVs in pig fibroblasts and produced PERV-free piglets.
Companies: Revivicor (United Therapeutics, Blacksburg VA): descends from PPL/Dolly.
10-gene-edited pig.
No PERV knockout — relies on barrier herd.
Made Bennett's heart.
eGenesis (Cambridge MA, founded by Church and Yang): 69-edit pig including PERV knockouts.
Made the 2024 kidney pigs for Slayman, Pisano, Looney.
Patients: David Bennett (January 7, 2022, University of Maryland, Bartley Griffith): first pig heart, 10-edit Revivicor donor, 60-day survival, died of porcine CMV reactivation that screening missed.
Lawrence Faucette (September 2023, Maryland): second pig heart, ~6 weeks, rejection.
Rick Slayman (March 16, 2024, Mass General, Tatsuo Kawai): first pig kidney, eGenesis 69-edit, 49-day survival, died of unrelated cardiac event with functioning kidney.
Lisa Pisano (April 12, 2024, NYU Langone, Robert Montgomery): pig kidney + pig thymus.
7-week kidney function, died of cardiac causes.
Towana Looney (November 2024, NYU Langone): longest survival; eGenesis kidney; still alive at last update, 4+ months.
Immunosuppression: Tacrolimus + belatacept (CD28 blockade) + anti-CD40L (costimulation blockade) + low-dose steroids.
Heavier than standard human-to-human protocol.
Layered T-cell blockade on top of edited organ.
FDA pathway: CBER regulates.
Current cases under expanded access / compassionate use, single-patient authorizations.
Formal Phase 1 IND trials expected to begin 2025.
First approvals expected ~2027-2028.
Lifelong patient surveillance for pig pathogens required.
Pig virus screening: Gnotobiotic herds — born by C-section into sterile isolators, never touch the outside world.
Sentinel testing.
Tissue-biopsy PCR for latent herpesviruses (the Bennett lesson — blood-based PCR missed pCMV).
Hep E, influenza A, S. suis also on the screening list.
Ethics: Religion: Islamic and Jewish authorities generally permit pig organs for life-saving use (darura / pikuach nefesh).
Hindu and Buddhist traditions similar.
Animal welfare: ~1 pig per organ.
High-welfare facilities.
Set against ~130 million pigs/year for US food.
Consent: Patients are no-options; debate over whether the desperate can truly consent.
Mitigated by extended consent process and ethics review.
Public health: Surveillance and contact restrictions to prevent zoonosis.
Competition: 3D bioprinting: vascularization is the unsolved problem; ~10-20 years behind xeno.
Stem-cell-derived organs (iPSC organoids): still millimeter-scale, scaling to organ-size unsolved.
Blastocyst complementation: grow human organs inside pigs by injecting human iPSCs into pig embryos with developmental knockouts.
Proof-of-concept demonstrated; not yet transplantable.
Frontier: more edits per pig, universal donor pigs, in-pig humanization (chimeras), formal Phase 1 trials in 2025, first approvals 2027-2028, routine clinical xeno by 2030s.

One-sentence summary: Genetic editing of pig genomes — knocking out alpha-gal and other xenoantigens, inserting human regulatory transgenes, inactivating endogenous retroviruses — has, after fifty years of failure, made pig organs survivable in humans, with Bennett in 2022 and Slayman/Pisano/Looney in 2024 as the proof of concept and routine clinical use plausible within a decade.
